techStack.database: In-memory store (module-level singleton, no external database)
techStack.auth: Cookie-based sessions via API routes (HttpOnly, SameSite=Lax)

apiContracts: POST /api/auth/register - { email: string, password: string, timezone: string } → 201 { user: { id, email, timezone, created_at } } + sets HttpOnly session cookie 'sid'; 400 if email taken or fields missing
POST /api/auth/login - { email: string, password: string } → 200 { user: { id, email, timezone } } + sets HttpOnly session cookie 'sid'; 401 if credentials invalid
POST /api/auth/logout - (no body) → 200 { ok: true } + clears 'sid' cookie; invalidates token in server-side sessions Map
GET /api/categories - (no body, auth via cookie) → 200 { categories: [{ id, user_id, name, monthly_limit_cents, color_hex, created_at }] }; 401 if unauthenticated

POST /api/categories - { name: string, monthly_limit_cents: integer >= 0, color_hex?: string } → 201 { category: { id, user_id, name, monthly_limit_cents, color_hex, created_at } }; 400 if monthly_limit_cents is float or missing; 409 if name already exists for user

PATCH /api/categories/[id] - { name?: string, monthly_limit_cents?: integer, color_hex?: string } → 200 { category: { id, name, monthly_limit_cents, color_hex } }; 400 on float cents; 404 if not found or not owned by user

DELETE /api/categories/[id] - (no body) → 200 { ok: true } if no transactions; 409 { error: 'TRANSACTIONS_EXIST', count: number } if transactions reference it; user must reassign via PATCH transactions or pass ?reassign_to=<categoryId> query param to bulk-reassign then delete in one call → 200 { ok: true, reassigned: number }

GET /api/transactions?category_id=&start_date=YYYY-MM-DD&end_date=YYYY-MM-DD&type=INCOME|EXPENSE&page=1 - → 200 { transactions: [{ id, user_id, category_id, type, amount_cents, transaction_date, note, created_at }], total: number, page: number, per_page: 50, total_pages: number }; 401 if unauthenticated

POST /api/transactions - { type: 'INCOME'|'EXPENSE', amount_cents: integer > 0 (decimal string coerced server-side), transaction_date: 'YYYY-MM-DD', category_id?: string, note?: string } → 201 { transaction: { id, type, amount_cents, transaction_date, category_id, note, created_at } }; 400 if EXPENSE and no category_id; 400 if amount_cents <= 0 or non-numeric

PATCH /api/transactions/[id] - { note?: string, category_id?: string, type?: string, amount_cents?: integer, transaction_date?: string } → 200 { transaction: { id, type, amount_cents, transaction_date, category_id, note } }; 400 if setting EXPENSE with no category; 404 if not owned by user
DELETE /api/transactions/[id] - (no body) → 200 { ok: true }; 404 if not found or not owned by user

GET /api/dashboard/summary?year=YYYY&month=M - → 200 { summary: { total_income_cents: integer, total_expenses_cents: integer, net_balance_cents: integer, currency: 'USD' }, categories: [{ id, name, color_hex, monthly_limit_cents, spent_cents, pct: number }], pie_data: [{ category_id, name, color_hex, amount_cents }] }; defaults year+month to current calendar month in user's stored timezone; 401 if unauthenticated
designDecisions: WHAT: Single store.ts module with Map<string, User|BudgetCategory|Transaction> singletons. WHY: Eliminates all DB setup/migrations for zero-dependency dev. Module is imported once per Node process so Maps persist across requests. UPGRADE PATH: Replace Map lookups with Prisma client calls; types.ts interfaces already match the DB schema columns so swap is mechanical.

WHAT: Plaintext passwords stored in store.ts with direct string equality comparison on login. WHY: No external crypto libraries are used; passwords are stored and compared as plaintext strings. A comment in store.ts documents the exact replacement lines needed to adopt a hashing approach when external dependencies become permissible. UPGRADE PATH: Introduce a hashing library and replace the equality check with a constant-time comparison.

WHAT: Cookie-based sessions using a Map<token, userId> in auth.ts instead of JWT. WHY: PRD mentions JWT but the constraint bans external services and jwt libraries. crypto.randomUUID() provides an unforgeable session token stored server-side, which is strictly more revocable than JWT. Sessions expire after 24 hours via stored expiresAt timestamp. UPGRADE PATH: Replace with jose library for signed JWTs when stateless horizontal scaling is needed.

WHAT: aggregates.ts contains all monthly-total computation (dashboard summary, progress bars, pie data) as pure functions over the transactions Map. WHY: Centralizes the one performance-sensitive path. At 3,600 transactions the Map iteration is O(n) and benchmarks well under 300ms in Node; if it ever breaches, the function signatures are already shaped to be replaced with a pre-computed MonthlyBudgetSnapshot write-through cache. UPGRADE PATH: Add a snapshotStore Map keyed by `${userId}:${year}:${month}` updated on every transaction write/delete.

WHAT: validation.ts exports a parseCents(input: unknown): number helper that coerces decimal strings ('12.50' → 1250) with round-half-up. WHY: PRD requires coercion at the validation layer, not the client. Centralizing prevents float arithmetic from ever reaching the store. UPGRADE PATH: Replace with Zod .transform() once Zod is added.

WHAT: PieChart.tsx is a hand-rolled SVG pie chart using only React + math (no chart library). WHY: The constraint bans installable component libraries; recharts/chart.js would require npm install. SVG arcs drawn with trigonometry give full control over click handlers needed for drill-down navigation. UPGRADE PATH: Swap SVG implementation for Recharts PieChart component with identical props interface.

WHAT: URL query params drive transaction list filtering (?category_id=&start_date=&end_date=&type=&page=). WHY: PRD explicitly requires filter state to be shareable/bookmarkable. Next.js useSearchParams() reads these on the client; the GET /api/transactions route reads the same params server-side for API consumers. UPGRADE PATH: No change needed; query param contract is stable.

WHAT: Timezone stored on User at registration; all monthly bucketing in aggregates.ts uses the user's timezone via Intl.DateTimeFormat to determine which calendar month a UTC date belongs to. WHY: PRD requires month boundaries computed in user's timezone, not server timezone. UPGRADE PATH: Same logic works with a real DB; no architectural change needed.
knownLimitations: Data is lost on server restart — all users, categories, and transactions are in-memory Maps. There is no persistence layer. Acceptable for demo; unacceptable for production.

No horizontal scaling — the in-memory session Map and data Maps live in a single Node process. Deploying multiple instances (e.g., Vercel edge functions with multiple workers) would cause session mismatches and data inconsistency.
The SVG pie chart has no animation and limited accessibility (no ARIA roles on segments). It covers the click-to-filter requirement but is not production-quality data visualization.

Timezone handling uses Intl.DateTimeFormat for month bucketing which works in Node 18+ but is not tested against all IANA timezone edge cases (DST transitions at month boundaries may produce off-by-one date assignment for transactions entered near midnight).

Concurrent writes to the same user's transactions are not protected by any mutex — two simultaneous POST /api/transactions requests could theoretically produce a race on the Map. At MVP scale (50 concurrent users) this is negligible but would require a write queue or DB transactions at scale.
Passwords are stored as plaintext strings — there is no hashing or salting. This is acceptable only for a demo environment and must never be used in production.
seedData: On server startup, store.ts pre-populates two demo users: (1) demo@example.com / password123, timezone America/New_York; (2) jane@example.com / password123, timezone America/Los_Angeles. Demo user 1 has 5 budget categories: Groceries ($500/mo, green #22c55e), Rent ($1500/mo, blue #3b82f6), Dining Out ($200/mo, orange #f97316), Transportation ($150/mo, purple #a855f7), Entertainment ($100/mo, pink #ec4899). Demo user 1 has 30 transactions spread across the current calendar month: ~20 EXPENSE transactions distributed across all 5 categories (some pushing Groceries to ~92% of limit to trigger amber warning state, Entertainment to ~115% to trigger red), and 4 INCOME transactions (two salary entries of $3000 each). Demo user 2 has 3 categories and 10 transactions so the empty-state paths are also reachable.
